🤖 Agentic workflow · Sysco

Smart Assignment

An AI agent that autonomously recommends the best delivery day & time slot for a new customer — running every step end-to-end, enforcing hard operational rules in code, ranking options on weighted business factors, and escalating to a human whenever the best option's own score falls short.

🤖 Fully agent-automated ✅ Deterministic, auditable decisions 🧠 LLM-written reasoning 🧪 Running on mock data
The problem

Onboarding a new account, every time, the same way

When a new customer signs on, someone has to decide which delivery route and time window they should join. That decision balances truck capacity, geography, and the customer's preference — and it needs to be consistent, explainable, and fast. The Smart Assignment agent makes that call automatically, and flags the tricky ones for a specialist instead of guessing.

How the agent works

Five steps, executed autonomously by the agent

🤖
A single AI agent performs all five stages below in one end-to-end run — these are phases of the same agent's workflow, not five separate agents. A person is involved only if the agent decides to escalate at the final stage.
1

Intake

Capture the customer's address, order quantity (cases), and preferred slot (day + time).

Validate the number & build the profile.

2

Geo-Lookup

Geocode the address and find the nearest candidate routes.

Geocode & pick the Top-3 nearest.

3

Constraint Check

Drop any route that fails a hard rule.

Enforce serviceability & capacity.

4

Score & Rank

Rank survivors on weighted business factors.

Score & order the options.

5

Recommend

Return the top slot with a reasoning trace — or escalate.

Check the total score & decide.

The rules the agent enforces

Hard constraints — non-negotiable, checked in code

Just two objective facts gate feasibility — not judgment calls. The agent removes any route that fails one before ranking; it can never "reason" a customer onto a full truck or outside the serviceable area. (See the Simulator tab for exactly how the surviving routes are scored.)

📍

Geographic serviceability

The customer must fall within the route's serviceable radius.

📦

Route capacity

The truck stays at or below 90% capacity after adding this order.

🕑 The preferred delivery slot — a day of week plus a time window — is a soft preference, not a hard constraint — it never eliminates a route. Instead it feeds the slot match scoring factor, so a route on a different day or time can still win if it's the best overall fit.

The outcome

Three possible agent decisions

Every run ends in one of three states. Anything whose own total score falls below 60%, or with no valid slot at all, the agent hands to a human — with full context attached.

✔ Recommended

A clear winner whose own total score clears the bar — the agent auto-assigns.

⚠ Low score

A slot is proposed, but its own total score falls short — the agent asks a specialist to confirm.

✖ No feasible slot

Every candidate failed a hard rule — the agent routes it to a specialist.

Architecture

The agentic workflow, end to end

The agent is a Google ADK Workflow that orchestrates the five steps. It calls deterministic tools for the objective checks and an LLM only to narrate its decision — the decision itself is code, so it's reproducible and auditable.

🤖

Agent orchestrator

An ADK Workflow drives all five steps and the branching, autonomously.

🧭

Deterministic tools

Geo, hard constraints, and weighted scoring — plain code the agent calls.

🧠

LLM reasoner

Gemini turns the agent's decision into a plain-English rationale (optional).

🙋

Human-in-the-loop

Only engaged when the agent escalates — low total score or no feasible slot.

Key mechanics

What makes it trustworthy

🤖 One agent, five steps

A single ADK Workflow runs Intake → Geo-Lookup → Constraint Check → Score & Rank → Decide, and branches to escalate.

✅ Decisions are code, not vibes

Constraints and scoring are deterministic Python, so identical inputs always yield the identical recommendation.

🧠 The LLM only narrates

Gemini writes the human-readable rationale; it never changes the decision, and falls back to a deterministic explanation.

🙋 Human-in-the-loop on escalation

A specialist is engaged only when the agent finds no feasible slot, or the best option's own total score falls short of the auto-assign bar.

How the agent scores & ranks

Exactly how each dimension is scored

Only routes that pass every hard rule reach this stage. The agent scores each on three dimensions, normalizes each to 0–1 (clamp keeps it in range), and combines them by weight. This is deterministic Python — the same inputs always give the same score.

🧭

Geographic clustering · weight 0.45

How tightly the customer sits within the route's existing cluster of stops. Closer = higher.

score = clamp( 1 − avg_miles_to_stops ÷ 15 , 0, 1 )

Distance is the average great-circle miles to the route's committed stops; at 15 mi the score reaches 0.

🛡️

Capacity buffer · weight 0.30

How safely under the capacity ceiling the truck stays once this order is added. Flat while comfortably safe; only decays as the truck approaches its limit.

score = 1.0 if utilization ≤ 75%
score = clamp( (90% − utilization) ÷ 15% , 0, 1 ) otherwise

Utilization stays flat at 1.0 up to 75% full (the 15%-point safety margin below the 90% ceiling); past that it falls straight to 0 exactly at the ceiling. Two routes that are both comfortably safe score the same — only a route that is genuinely getting full is marked down.

🎯

Slot match (day + time) · weight 0.25

How well the route matches the customer's preferred slot — which always includes a day of week plus a time-of-day window. A soft preference: it shapes the score but never eliminates a route.

score = 0 if route_day ≠ preferred_day, or zero time overlap
score = clamp( overlap_minutes ÷ preferred_window_minutes , 0, 1 ) otherwise

The day is a gate, not partial credit: a route only earns any score once it lands on the customer's preferred day, and then only for however much of the preferred window it actually covers. Wrong day, or right day with no time overlap, scores 0 — half-right isn't a match. If the customer states no slot, a neutral 0.60 is used instead.

Final score, then the auto-assign decision

The overall score is the weighted average of the three dimensions:

total_score = ( 0.45·clustering + 0.30·capacity + 0.25·window ) ÷ 1.00

The agent ranks feasible routes by total_score and recommends the winner. That same number is what gates the decision — there's no separate "confidence" formula on top of it. The agent auto-assigns when the winner's own total score is ≥ 60%; otherwise it escalates for a specialist to review.

By design, a route's own score is never discounted just because another candidate scored nearly as well — two routes tied at a high score both clear the bar, and either is a safe pick. A route only gets flagged when its own score is mediocre, not because it happens to have close competition.

Where the numbers come from

Every threshold, and its source

The formulas above use real values from three places. Config defaults are tunable in shared/config.py; per-route numbers (capacity, radius) are mock data standing in for a real routing system; the rest comes from the customer's intake.

⚙️ Config defaults shared/config.py

  • Route capacity ceiling — max_utilization_after_assignment90%
  • Capacity safety margin (safe up to) — capacity_buffer_safety_margin15% pts (→ 75%)
  • Clustering reference (score→0) — cluster_reference_miles15 mi
  • No-window neutral score — window_neutral_score0.60
  • Scoring weights (geo/cap/win) — factor_weights0.45 / 0.30 / 0.25
  • Total score threshold (auto-assign bar) — total_score_threshold60%
  • Serviceability hard cap — max_service_distance_miles25 mi
  • Candidates evaluated — top_n_candidate_routesTop-3

🗄️ Route data mocked TMS

  • Vehicle capacity per route — denominator of utilization %700–950 cases
  • Service radius per route — the serviceability limit12–16 mi
  • Committed stops + locations — drive clustering & used capacityvaries
  • Available delivery windows — window overlapvaries

From integrations/route_capacity_client.py.

🧾 Customer intake new customer

  • Address → geocoded point — geocodingper customer
  • Order quantity — capacity mathcases
  • Preferred slot: day + time (optional) — slot scoring onlysoft

Env vars can override any config default (e.g. SMART_ASSIGNMENT_MAX_UTILIZATION).

Try it yourself

Run the agent on a customer

Enter a mock customer number (or pick one below) and watch the agent execute each step — including the scoring math for every feasible route — then render its recommendation. Everything runs in your browser; no data leaves the page.

Sample customer numbers — click one to load it into the box above, then press Run agent workflow:
All outcomes at a glance

What the agent decided for each mock customer

These cards are generated straight from the agent's output. Each customer lands on a different outcome; expand “Routes the agent evaluated” to audit the full decision.

067-100001

Bayou City Bistro

1200 McKinney St, Houston, TX 77010
90 cases prefers TUE 07:00–10:00 3 routes evaluated
✔ Recommended total score 97%

→ RTE-4100 · Central Houston · TUE · 07:00–10:00 (score 0.97)

Geographic clustering
0.94
Capacity buffer
1.00
Slot match (day + time)
1.00
Why the agent chose thisFor Bayou City Bistro, I recommend route RTE-4100 (Central Houston), delivering on Tuesday between 07:00-10:00. Geographically, it sits right in the middle of the stops already on this route — avg 0.8 mi to existing stops. On capacity, the truck still has plenty of room to spare after this order — 290 cases of headroom left, putting the truck at about 68% full after this order (comfortably safe up to 75%). On timing, both the day and the time line up exactly with what the customer asked for — the route runs on Tuesday, matching the customer's preference, and covers 180 of the 180 minutes of their preferred time. It was also the only route that cleared every requirement, so there wasn't anything else to weigh it against. Putting all of that together, this pick earns a total score of 97% — comfortably above the 60% bar I use before auto-assigning, so I'm comfortable moving ahead without a specialist review.
Routes the agent evaluated (3)
RTE-4100 · Central Houston · TUE · 0.1 miFEASIBLE · 0.97
  • ✓ service area: 0.1 mi from route center (limit 12.0 mi)
  • ✓ truck capacity: utilization 68% post-add (limit 90%), 290 cases headroom
RTE-4200 · West Houston / Energy Corridor · WED · 14.7 miINFEASIBLE
  • ✗ service area: 14.7 mi from route center (limit 12.0 mi)
  • ✓ truck capacity: utilization 52% post-add (limit 90%), 460 cases headroom
RTE-4400 · Southwest / Sugar Land · TUE · 18.7 miINFEASIBLE
  • ✗ service area: 18.7 mi from route center (limit 12.0 mi)
  • ✗ truck capacity: utilization 101% post-add (limit 90%), -10 cases headroom
067-100002

Galleria Grill & Catering

5085 Westheimer Rd, Houston, TX 77056
400 cases no slot preference 3 routes evaluated
⚠ Low score — human review total score 57%
below the auto-assign bar

→ proposed: RTE-4200 · West Houston / Energy Corridor · WED · 07:30–11:00 (score 0.57)

Geographic clustering
0.67
Capacity buffer
0.39
Slot match (day + time)
0.60
Why the agent escalatedFor Galleria Grill & Catering, I recommend route RTE-4200 (West Houston / Energy Corridor), delivering on Wednesday between 07:30-11:00. Geographically, it lines up reasonably well with the stops already on this route — avg 5.0 mi to existing stops. The customer did not name a preferred day or time, so I treated every option evenly on that front — no stated preference (neutral score). On capacity, the truck is getting quite full for this order — there's still some room, but not a lot of cushion — 150 cases of headroom left, putting the truck at about 84% full after this order (comfortably safe up to 75%). It was also the only route that cleared every requirement, so there wasn't anything else to weigh it against. Putting all of that together, this pick's total score comes out to 57%, which falls short of the 60% bar I use before auto-assigning. Rather than commit on my own, I'd like a specialist to take a quick look before this goes out.
Routes the agent evaluated (3)
RTE-4100 · Central Houston · TUE · 5.9 miINFEASIBLE
  • ✓ service area: 5.9 mi from route center (limit 12.0 mi)
  • ✗ truck capacity: utilization 102% post-add (limit 90%), -20 cases headroom
RTE-4200 · West Houston / Energy Corridor · WED · 9.3 miFEASIBLE · 0.57
  • ✓ service area: 9.3 mi from route center (limit 12.0 mi)
  • ✓ truck capacity: utilization 84% post-add (limit 90%), 150 cases headroom
RTE-4400 · Southwest / Sugar Land · TUE · 13.3 miINFEASIBLE
  • ✗ service area: 13.3 mi from route center (limit 12.0 mi)
  • ✗ truck capacity: utilization 146% post-add (limit 90%), -320 cases headroom
067-100003

Katy Prairie Steakhouse

24600 Katy Fwy, Katy, TX 77494
260 cases prefers TUE 06:00–08:00 3 routes evaluated
✖ No feasible slot — specialist total score 0%
no candidate passed the hard rules
Why the agent escalatedI looked at every nearby route for Katy Prairie Steakhouse, and none of them could take this order: route RTE-4200 on Wednesday didn't work because of service area; route RTE-4400 on Tuesday didn't work because of service area and truck capacity; route RTE-4100 on Tuesday didn't work because of service area. Since nothing cleared the basic requirements, I'm escalating this to a routing specialist rather than force a fit. This will most likely need a manual decision, such as opening a new route, shifting a schedule, or freeing up capacity somewhere else.
Routes the agent evaluated (3)
RTE-4200 · West Houston / Energy Corridor · WED · 12.8 miINFEASIBLE
  • ✗ service area: 12.8 mi from route center (limit 12.0 mi)
  • ✓ truck capacity: utilization 69% post-add (limit 90%), 290 cases headroom
RTE-4400 · Southwest / Sugar Land · TUE · 16.0 miINFEASIBLE
  • ✗ service area: 16.0 mi from route center (limit 12.0 mi)
  • ✗ truck capacity: utilization 126% post-add (limit 90%), -180 cases headroom
RTE-4100 · Central Houston · TUE · 27.4 miINFEASIBLE
  • ✗ service area: 27.4 mi from route center (limit 12.0 mi)
  • ✓ truck capacity: utilization 87% post-add (limit 90%), 120 cases headroom
067-100004

Woodlands Fresh Cafe

1201 Lake Woodlands Dr, The Woodlands, TX 77380
150 cases prefers THU 09:00–12:00 3 routes evaluated
✔ Recommended total score 86%

→ RTE-4300 · North Houston / The Woodlands · THU · 08:00–12:00 (score 0.86)

Geographic clustering
0.96
Capacity buffer
0.58
Slot match (day + time)
1.00
Why the agent chose thisFor Woodlands Fresh Cafe, I recommend route RTE-4300 (North Houston / The Woodlands), delivering on Thursday between 08:00-12:00. Geographically, it sits right in the middle of the stops already on this route — avg 0.6 mi to existing stops. On timing, both the day and the time line up exactly with what the customer asked for — the route runs on Thursday, matching the customer's preference, and covers 180 of the 180 minutes of their preferred time. On capacity, the truck is getting fuller, though it stays inside a comfortable enough margin — 150 cases of headroom left, putting the truck at about 81% full after this order (comfortably safe up to 75%). It was also the only route that cleared every requirement, so there wasn't anything else to weigh it against. Putting all of that together, this pick earns a total score of 86% — comfortably above the 60% bar I use before auto-assigning, so I'm comfortable moving ahead without a specialist review.
Routes the agent evaluated (3)
RTE-4300 · North Houston / The Woodlands · THU · 0.3 miFEASIBLE · 0.86
  • ✓ service area: 0.3 mi from route center (limit 16.0 mi)
  • ✓ truck capacity: utilization 81% post-add (limit 90%), 150 cases headroom

  • ✓ truck capacity: utilization 58% post-add (limit 90%), 400 cases headroom
RTE-4100 · Central Houston · TUE · 28.4 miINFEASIBLE

  • ✓ truck capacity: utilization 74% post-add (limit 90%), 230 cases headroom
About this data. The agent runs on mock Houston-area routes and geocoding so the workflow can be demonstrated end-to-end. Capacities, service radii, scoring weights, and thresholds are illustrative starting points — not validated Sysco policy. The route source and geocoder are designed to be swapped for real systems without changing the agent's logic.
Smart Assignment · an AI agent for delivery slot recommendation · built on Google's Agent Development Kit (ADK).
Source & docs on GitHub. Decisions are deterministic and auditable; reasoning narration is LLM-written with a deterministic fallback.
